checks.message: `unwrap_or_else(|_| ...)` ignores the error and substitutes a default. If the error path is provably unreachable use `.expect("invariant X because ...")` so a future regression panics loudly with context. If it IS reachable, handle it properly — don't silently swap in a default.

`// SAFETY:` comment but the file contains no `unsafe` block. SAFETY markers are reserved for justifying `unsafe { ... }` / `unsafe fn`. On safe code they are misleading noise — reviewers must re-verify there's no hidden unsafe, and future readers assume the author meant something. Delete the marker or move it next to the real unsafe site.

`.unwrap()` in production code panics on error. In tests this is idiomatic; in lib/ or main code, prefer `?`, `.expect("invariant")`, or a typed error. (Note: this check fires conservatively — skipped for files with a test-scoped allow or under tests/ / benches/ / examples/.)

`let _ = <fallible io op>` silently discards the error. If the error path is provably unreachable, `.expect("invariant")` records WHY. If it IS reachable, handle it. The bug class: an edge-case error becomes silent garbage instead of a loud diagnostic.

`Duration::from_*(N)` literal outside a policy/config file. If this value encodes a deliberate timeout/interval, it belongs in `policy.rs` / `config.rs` / `constants.rs` as a named `const` so the SSOT litmus holds (`grep '<NAME>'` finds every caller). Literals in business logic drift. Integration-test fixtures under `tests/` / `benches/` are exempt — their durations are empirical orchestration values, not policy.

checks.cite: rust-reviewing (Rusty_BEAM_node M20-M24 review, finding 12): SPAWN_REPLY Result must be a bare atom
checks.message: `build_spawn_reply(... Term::Tuple(vec![Term::atom("error"|"ok"), ...]))` — SPAWN_REPLY's Result field must be a bare reason atom on failure (e.g. `Term::atom("undef")`) or a `Term::Pid(...)` on success. OTP validates the tuple shape strictly and treats `{error, Reason}` tuples as "Invalid distribution message" — it drops the connection. Use the bare atom; the caller's distribution layer wraps it into `{spawn_reply, Ref, error, Reason}` locally.

checks.cite: rust-reviewing (Rusty_BEAM_node M20-M24 review, finding 2): tokio::spawn + user closure + post-cleanup without panic guard
checks.message: `tokio::spawn(async move { ... handler(args) ...; cleanup; })` runs user-provided closure code inline and does cleanup after. A panic inside `handler(args)` unwinds the whole task — cleanup never fires, resources leak, remote callers waiting on a completion signal hang. Route the user closure through `tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || handler(args)).await` so a panic becomes `JoinError::is_panic()` that the cleanup code can observe, then still run cleanup. Also benefits sync handlers (doesn't stall the async runtime). Checked at file scope — silenced when the file already mentions spawn_blocking / catch_unwind / AssertUnwindSafe.

NIF function returns `Vec<u8>` — Rustler encodes this as a *list of integers*, not a binary. Use `NewBinary::new(env, len)` + `as_mut_slice().copy_from_slice(&bytes)` and return `Binary<'a>`, OR `OwnedBinary::new(n).release(env)` for the older API. See rust-nif Rule 18 / §2.3 #2.

checks.cite: rust-nif §2.8 — Error::Term encodes as {:error, _}; only Error::RaiseTerm/RaiseAtom raise
checks.message: `rustler::Error::Term(Box::new(_))` *encodes* as `{:error, encoded}` — it does NOT raise on the Elixir side. If you want a NIF returning `NifResult<T>` to raise on Err (the typical bang-style API), use `Error::RaiseTerm(Box::new(...))` or `Error::RaiseAtom("...")`. If you want `{:error, _}` tuples on the Elixir side, change the function's return type to `Result<T, MyError>` (with `Encoder` for `MyError`) — `NifResult<T>` is for raises. The hook silences once the file also mentions RaiseTerm/RaiseAtom (assumes intentional mix).

`#[module = "Elixir.X"]` on a NifStruct produces `__struct__: Elixir.Elixir.X` — rustler auto-prepends `Elixir.`. Drop the prefix: `#[module = "MyApp.X"]`. Note the asymmetry that trips people up: `rustler::init!("Elixir.MyApp.Native")` DOES need the prefix; the NifStruct attribute does NOT.
`gets()` was removed in C11 because there is no way to use it safely. Use `fgets()` with an explicit buffer size, or POSIX `getline()`.
`strcpy()` has no bounds checking. Prefer `strncpy` with explicit size (and manual null-termination), `strlcpy` where available, or `snprintf`.
